seo.title: How to Run Payroll in Jamaica (2026): Step-by-Step Guide
seo.description: A plain-English guide to running payroll in Jamaica in 2026. Covers PAYE, NIS, NHT registration, calculating deductions, and paying employees on time.
keywords: how to run payroll in Jamaica

title: How to Run Payroll in Jamaica (2026): Step-by-Step Guide
subtitle: Everything a first-time employer needs to register, calculate deductions, and pay employees correctly — without the jargon.

ctaInline.headline: Skip the Spreadsheets
ctaInline.text: Use our free Jamaica payroll calculator to get accurate deductions instantly

ctaBottom.headline: Make payroll effortless
ctaBottom.text: PayrollJamaica handles PAYE, NIS, NHT and payslips automatically — try it free

html:
Congratulations — you're about to hire your first employee. That's a big deal for any small business in Jamaica. But if you've started googling "how to run payroll" and found yourself drowning in acronyms like PAYE, NIS, NHT and TAJ, you're not alone. Every employer starts exactly where you are right now.

The good news: once you've done the setup, payroll becomes a reliable monthly routine. This guide walks you through every step, in plain English, so you can pay your employee correctly and stay on the right side of Jamaican tax law from day one.

Step 1: Register with the Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ)

Before you can legally deduct PAYE (Pay As You Earn income tax) from an employee's salary, you need to be registered as an employer with the Tax Administration Jamaica.

What you'll need first: a TRN

Your business must have a Tax Registration Number (TRN). If you're operating as a sole trader, your personal TRN can work. If your business is registered as a company, the company needs its own TRN. You can apply for a TRN at any TAJ office or online at jamaicatax.gov.jm.

Register as an employer

Once you have a TRN, register as an employer with TAJ. You'll receive an Employer Registration Number, which you'll use on all monthly statutory filings. Registration can be done in person at any TAJ office island-wide, or through their online portal. Bring your TRN, business registration certificate, and a valid photo ID.

This registration is what allows TAJ to track your monthly PAYE remittances. Skipping this step — or delaying it — can result in penalties that far outweigh the hassle of just getting it done upfront.

Step 2: Register with NIS

The National Insurance Scheme (NIS) is Jamaica's social security programme, administered by the Ministry of Labour and Social Security. As an employer, you're legally required to register your business and enrol every employee.

Why it matters

NIS contributions fund benefits for your employees including sickness benefit, maternity benefit, retirement pension, and survivor's benefit. Both you and your employee contribute — meaning the cost is shared.

The current NIS contribution rates

  • Employee contribution: 3% of insurable earnings
  • Employer contribution: 3% of insurable earnings

The NIS ceiling for 2025/2026 is J$3,000,000 in annual insurable earnings. Contributions are not required on earnings above that ceiling.

How to register

Visit any NIS office with your business registration documents and TRN. Your employee will also need their own NIS number — if they don't have one yet, they can apply at the same office. Once registered, you'll receive an employer NIS number used on all filings.

Step 3: Register with NHT

The National Housing Trust (NHT) is a statutory body that helps Jamaicans access affordable housing loans. Both employers and employees contribute, and employees can access their NHT benefits after making contributions for a qualifying period.

NHT contribution rates

  • Employee contribution: 2% of gross earnings
  • Employer contribution: 3% of gross earnings

Unlike NIS, there is no ceiling on NHT contributions — they apply to the full gross salary regardless of how high it goes.

Registering with NHT

You can register your business with the NHT at any NHT office or online at nht.gov.jm. Bring your TRN, certificate of registration, and your employer NIS number. Each employee also needs to be registered individually with their own NHT number.

Don't forget: Education Tax is another statutory deduction collected alongside NHT contributions. Your employee pays 2.25% of gross earnings, and you as the employer pay 3.5% — these are remitted to TAJ together with PAYE on the same monthly deadline.

Step 4: Calculate Your Deductions

This is where most first-time employers get nervous — but the calculation follows a logical sequence. Once you understand the order of operations, it becomes straightforward.

The 2025/2026 PAYE threshold

The annual income tax threshold for 2025/2026 is J$1,825,000. This means an employee earning up to J$1,825,000 per year pays no income tax at all. That works out to approximately J$152,083 per month before PAYE kicks in.

For earnings above the threshold, the standard income tax rate is 25%. There is an additional tax rate of 30% on chargeable income above J$6,000,000 annually, but that won't apply to most small business employees.

Worked example: Employee earning J$80,000 per month

Let's say you hire a full-time employee with a gross monthly salary of J$80,000. Here is exactly how to calculate their take-home pay and your employer obligations.

Step A — Calculate statutory deductions from gross pay

Deduction Rate Calculation Amount
NIS (employee) 3% J$80,000 × 3% J$2,400
NHT (employee) 2% J$80,000 × 2% J$1,600
Education Tax (employee) 2.25% J$80,000 × 2.25% J$1,800

Step B — Calculate PAYE

PAYE is calculated on chargeable income, which is gross pay minus NIS contributions (NIS is the only pre-tax deduction allowed before PAYE is computed).

  • Gross monthly pay: J$80,000
  • Less NIS: J$2,400
  • Monthly chargeable income: J$77,600

Now compare this to the monthly tax threshold:

  • Annual threshold: J$1,825,000 ÷ 12 = J$152,083/month
  • J$77,600 is below J$152,083
  • Therefore: PAYE = J$0 (this employee earns below the threshold)

Great news for your employee — and for you as a new employer, this means no PAYE to remit to TAJ for this particular staff member this month.

Step C — Summary of deductions from employee pay

Item Amount
Gross Pay J$80,000.00
Less NIS (3%) − J$2,400.00
Less NHT (2%) − J$1,600.00
Less Education Tax (2.25%) − J$1,800.00
Less PAYE − J$0.00
Net Take-Home Pay J$74,200.00

Step D — Your employer contributions (on top of gross pay)

Remember: as the employer, you also pay your own share of statutory contributions. These are your costs in addition to the employee's salary — they are not deducted from the employee's pay.

Employer Contribution Rate Amount
NIS (employer share) 3% J$2,400.00
NHT (employer share) 3% J$2,400.00
Education Tax (employer share) 3.5% J$2,800.00
Total Employer Statutory Cost J$7,600.00

So for this employee, your total monthly cost is J$80,000 (salary) + J$7,600 (employer statutory contributions) = J$87,600 per month. That's the true cost of the hire — worth knowing before you commit to the salary.

Want to skip these manual calculations entirely? Use our Jamaica payroll calculator — just enter the gross salary and it handles every deduction automatically.

Step 5: Process and Distribute Payslips

Every employee in Jamaica has the right to a payslip — a written record of their gross pay, every deduction made, and their net take-home pay. This is both a legal best practice and a trust-builder with your team.

What a payslip must include

  • Employee name and NIS/NHT number
  • Pay period (e.g., March 1–31, 2026)
  • Gross pay broken down by type (basic salary, overtime, allowances)
  • Each statutory deduction itemised (NIS, NHT, Education Tax, PAYE)
  • Any other deductions (loan repayments, union dues, etc.)
  • Net pay
  • Payment method (cash, cheque, or direct deposit)

Paying your employee

Most small businesses in Jamaica pay employees by direct bank transfer. Leading banks NCB (National Commercial Bank) and Scotiabank Jamaica both support direct deposit integration — meaning you can authorise a batch payment from your business account directly into your employee's account on payday. This eliminates the security risk of carrying cash and creates an automatic paper trail for your records.

If you're running payroll manually, prepare a payment file with your employee's account number and branch sort code and submit it through your business banking portal before the cutoff time (typically midday the business day before payday).

Set a consistent payday — whether monthly or fortnightly — and stick to it. Jamaica's labour law requires wages to be paid regularly and on time. Late payments can expose you to complaints under the Employment (Termination and Redundancy Payments) Act and damage the trust you're building with your team.

Step 6: File Your Monthly Returns

Collecting statutory deductions from your employee is only half the job. You must also remit those deductions — plus your employer contributions — to the relevant government agencies every month. Missing a deadline or sending the wrong amount triggers interest and penalties that can add up quickly.

Monthly filing deadline: the 14th of each month

All statutory deductions and employer contributions must be filed and paid by the 14th of the month following payroll. So if you pay your employee on March 31, the March deductions are due by April 14.

What you file and where

  • PAYE & Education Tax → Filed to TAJ using the monthly Employer's Return (S02 form). Submit online via the TAJ e-Services portal or in person at any TAJ office.
  • NIS contributions → Remit employee and employer contributions to NIS (Ministry of Labour) using the NIS Contribution Schedule. You can pay at any NIS office, approved commercial banks, or online via some banking portals.
  • NHT contributions → Filed and paid to NHT using the Contribution Schedule. Submit through NHT's online portal or at any NHT branch.

Annual requirements

In addition to monthly filings, you must submit an Annual Employer's Return to TAJ by March 31 each year. This summarises all salaries paid and all PAYE deducted for the full tax year. You'll also need to issue each employee a TD4 certificate (similar to a W-2 in the US) showing their annual earnings and tax deducted — this is what they use to file their own personal income tax return.

Penalties for late filing

TAJ charges interest at 16.62% per annum on overdue PAYE, compounded monthly. There are also fixed penalties for failing to file returns on time. The simplest way to avoid these costs is to set a recurring calendar reminder for the 10th of every month — giving yourself a buffer before the 14th deadline to gather figures, calculate, and submit.

The Easier Way: Use Payroll Software

If all of the above sounds like a lot to manage alongside actually running your business — you're right. Manually tracking deductions, preparing filings, and generating payslips every month takes time that most small business owners simply don't have.

This is exactly why payroll software exists. A good Jamaica-specific payroll platform will:

  • Automatically calculate PAYE, NIS, NHT and Education Tax for every employee using the latest 2025/2026 rates and the J$1,825,000 threshold
  • Generate professional payslips in one click — ready to email directly to your employee
  • Produce the monthly filing summaries you need for TAJ, NIS and NHT so you're not building spreadsheets from scratch
  • Keep a full audit trail of every pay run, deduction, and filing — exactly what TAJ wants to see if they ever audit you
  • Handle direct deposit integration with NCB and Scotiabank, so payroll funds hit employee accounts automatically on payday
  • Send you reminders before the 14th-of-month deadline so nothing slips through the cracks

For a single employee, the manual approach is workable — but it's surprisingly easy to make a mistake in the PAYE calculation order, miss a filing, or use an outdated rate. Each of those mistakes costs real money in penalties. Software removes the guesswork entirely.

Start with the calculator

Not ready to commit to software yet? Start by running your numbers through our free Jamaica payroll calculator. Enter your employee's gross salary, and it instantly shows you the exact PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax breakdown — both the employee deductions and your employer contributions. It uses the current 2025/2026 statutory rates and threshold, so the numbers are always accurate.

Quick Reference: Jamaica Payroll Rates 2025/2026

Deduction Employee Rate Employer Rate Remitted To
PAYE (Income Tax) 25% on income above threshold N/A TAJ
NIS 3% 3% NIS (Min. of Labour)
NHT 2% 3% NHT
Education Tax 2.25% 3.5% TAJ
PAYE Threshold (annual) J$1,825,000 —
Monthly filing deadline 14th of the following month TAJ / NIS / NHT

Your Payroll Setup Checklist

Before you process your very first payroll run, make sure you've completed all of these:

  • Business TRN obtained from TAJ
  • Employer registered with TAJ (Employer Registration Number received)
  • Business registered as employer with NIS
  • Business registered with NHT
  • Employee's TRN, NIS number, and NHT number collected
  • Employee's bank account details collected for direct deposit
  • Payroll calculation method confirmed (manual spreadsheet or software)
  • Payday set and communicated to employee
  • Monthly filing reminder set for the 10th of each month

Running payroll in Jamaica for the first time feels intimidating — but as you can see, it's a series of logical steps. Register with the right agencies, calculate the deductions in the right order, pay your employee on time, and file by the 14th. Do that consistently, and you're a fully compliant employer.

When you're ready to remove the manual work entirely, PayrollJamaica is built specifically for Jamaican small businesses. It handles every calculation, every payslip, every filing summary — and integrates directly with NCB and Scotiabank for seamless direct deposit. Start with the free Jamaica payroll calculator and see how simple payroll can actually be.
